agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "A global logistics platform adding real-time driver earnings transparency across three jurisdictions with fundamentally different legal frameworks for driver classification, wage disclosure, and labor governance. This is not one feature — it is three jurisdiction-specific implementations unified by a single UI intent.",
    "bounded_context": "Driver Compensation / Earnings Visibility",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Base Pay", "definition": "The guaranteed minimum compensation for accepting and completing a delivery, before bonuses, tips, or deductions. For employees (CA), this must meet or exceed minimum wage floor. For contractors (ID), this is a platform-defined rate." },
      { "term": "Per-Delivery Bonus", "definition": "Variable compensation tied to completing individual deliveries, potentially including surge pricing, distance bonuses, or batch incentives." },
      { "term": "Tips", "definition": "Voluntary gratuities from customers passed through to the driver. In California, tips must never be counted toward base pay or minimum wage calculations (Labor Code §351)." },
      { "term": "Deductions", "definition": "Amounts subtracted from gross earnings. CRITICAL: this term means fundamentally different things by jurisdiction. For CA employees: taxes, benefits, garnishments (governed by Labor Code §226). For ID contractors: platform fees, commissions, equipment charges." },
      { "term": "Earnings Breakdown", "definition": "An itemized view of all components contributing to a driver's compensation for a given period. In CA, this is a legal document equivalent to an itemized wage statement." },
      { "term": "Itemized Wage Statement", "definition": "California-specific: a legally mandated document under Labor Code §226 that must include gross wages, total hours, piece rates, all deductions, net wages, pay period dates, employer name/address. Penalties for non-compliance: $50 first violation, $100 subsequent, per employee per pay period." },
      { "term": "Works Council (Betriebsrat)", "definition": "Germany-specific: an elected employee representative body with co-determination rights under the Works Constitution Act (BetrVG). Has legal authority to approve or block changes to systems that monitor or display employee performance and compensation data (§87(1) No.6 BetrVG)." },
      { "term": "Real-Time", "definition": "Display updated after each delivery completion or earning event. CAUTION: 'real-time' display of partial earnings must not be confused with or substitute for legally required periodic wage statements." },
      { "term": "Driver Classification", "definition": "The legal relationship between the driver and the platform — employee (CA under AB5, Germany) or independent contractor (Indonesia). This classification determines which labor laws apply and what the platform is legally obligated to disclose." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Tips must never be included in or offset against base pay calculations in California. Tips are the sole property of the driver.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "California Labor Code §351 — violation is wage theft, a criminal offense. This is a legal constraint, not a policy choice.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a CA driver earning base pay of $X and tips of $Y, the base pay line item must always be >= applicable minimum wage × hours worked, calculated WITHOUT any tip amount."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "The California earnings breakdown must include all fields required by an itemized wage statement under Labor Code §226: gross wages earned, total hours worked, piece-rate units (deliveries), all deductions with descriptions, net wages, inclusive dates of the pay period, employee name and last four of SSN, employer legal name and address.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Statutory requirement. Non-compliance triggers per-employee per-period penalties and potential class action liability. The system cannot legally display partial information.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a CA driver views their earnings breakdown, THEN all §226-mandated fields are present and populated. No field may be null, blank, or omitted."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "The Germany earnings display system MUST NOT be deployed until the applicable Works Council (Betriebsrat) has formally approved the system under §87(1) No.6 BetrVG.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Works council co-determination is a legal requirement, not a courtesy. Deploying without approval is a violation of the Works Constitution Act and can result in an injunction halting the feature entirely.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given the Germany market, WHEN the feature toggle for earnings transparency is evaluated, THEN it returns disabled UNLESS a works_council_approval record exists with status=approved and an approval_date on or before the current date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Deductions displayed to employees (CA, Germany) must be legally categorized and distinguishable from deductions displayed to independent contractors (Indonesia). Employee deductions include statutory withholdings (taxes, social insurance). Contractor deductions include platform fees and commissions.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Displaying contractor-style deductions (e.g., 'platform commission') to employees implies misclassification. Displaying employee-style deductions (e.g., 'income tax withholding') to contractors implies the platform is acting as an employer. Either direction creates legal exposure.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a CA or DE driver, deduction line items must only contain employee-type deductions. Given an ID driver, deduction line items must only contain contractor-type deductions. No cross-contamination of deduction categories."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Earnings data must be scoped to the authenticated driver. A driver must never see another driver's earnings, partial or complete.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Data isolation is a fundamental system integrity constraint. Leaking compensation data violates privacy law in all three jurisdictions (CCPA, GDPR/BDSG, Indonesian PDP Law).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given driver A is authenticated, WHEN earnings data is fetched, THEN every record returned has driver_id = A. Zero records for any other driver_id may be included."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The earnings breakdown must reflect the driver's jurisdiction-specific classification. The system must determine jurisdiction from the driver's operating market, not from user preference or self-declaration.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Allowing drivers to self-select jurisdiction could enable displaying a less-regulated view, creating compliance gaps. Classification is a legal fact tied to operating market.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver registered in the CA market, WHEN their earnings view is rendered, THEN the employee-type template is used regardless of any user-level settings."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Real-time earnings display must carry a disclaimer that it is an estimate and does not replace the official pay statement (for employees) or invoice (for contractors).",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a risk-mitigation policy. A VP could decide the real-time view IS the official statement if it meets all legal requirements, though that would be inadvisable.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a driver views real-time earnings, THEN a disclaimer is visible indicating the figures are preliminary and subject to adjustment."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "In Germany, the earnings display must comply with GDPR data minimization — only compensation data necessary for transparency purposes may be displayed, and data retention for the display cache must be limited.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR compliance is a legal obligation, not a preference. Displaying unnecessary personal data or retaining it beyond purpose violates Article 5(1)(c).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a DE driver's earnings view, THEN only earnings-related fields are displayed; no extraneous personal data (e.g., full address, tax ID) appears in the real-time view."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A driver in California completes a delivery",
      "when": "They open the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips (separated from base pay), itemized deductions (tax withholding, benefits, etc.), net earnings, hours worked, and pay period dates — all fields required by Labor Code §226"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A driver in Germany and no works council approval exists for the earnings display feature",
      "when": "The feature is evaluated for rollout",
      "then": "The feature is disabled and inaccessible to all Germany-market drivers. No partial rollout, no beta, no A/B test."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A driver in Indonesia completes a delivery",
      "when": "They open the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and deductions labeled as platform fees/commissions (contractor terminology). No tax withholding or employer-style deductions appear."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A driver in California with base pay that, combined with hours worked, falls below minimum wage",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown is calculated",
      "then": "A minimum wage adjustment line item is automatically added to bring total hourly compensation (excluding tips) to at least the applicable minimum wage"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "Any driver in any jurisdiction",
      "when": "The earnings breakdown API is called",
      "then": "The response contains ONLY earnings records belonging to the authenticated driver's ID. No data leakage across drivers."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A driver in Germany and works council approval has been granted",
      "when": "They open the earnings breakdown screen",
      "then": "They see base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, itemized deductions (social insurance, income tax, solidarity surcharge as applicable), and net earnings — with GDPR-compliant data minimization"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A driver views real-time earnings mid-shift",
      "when": "Earnings are still accumulating and not yet finalized",
      "then": "The display clearly labels all figures as preliminary/estimated and references the official statement for final amounts"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A driver operates in both California and another US state (e.g., near state borders)", "expected_behavior": "Earnings must be attributed to the jurisdiction where each delivery was performed, not the driver's home address. Each delivery's jurisdiction determines the applicable rules for that delivery's line items." },
    { "scenario": "Germany works council approval is revoked after initial rollout", "expected_behavior": "The feature must be immediately disabled for all Germany-market drivers. The system must support approval revocation, not just initial approval. This is a kill switch, not a one-time gate." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver receives a tip adjustment (customer modifies tip post-delivery)", "expected_behavior": "The real-time display must update to reflect the adjusted tip. The adjustment must be logged as a separate line item for audit trail purposes, not silently modified." },
    { "scenario": "Platform fees change mid-shift for an Indonesian contractor", "expected_behavior": "Deliveries completed before the fee change use the old rate; deliveries after use the new rate. The breakdown must show each delivery with the fee rate applicable at the time of completion." },
    { "scenario": "A California driver works a shift with zero deliveries but was logged in and available", "expected_behavior": "If the driver is classified as an employee under AB5 and was required to be available, minimum wage for hours worked may still apply. The earnings screen must show guaranteed minimum pay even with zero delivery bonuses." },
    { "scenario": "Currency and locale differences across jurisdictions", "expected_behavior": "California displays in USD, Germany in EUR, Indonesia in IDR. Formatting follows local conventions (decimal separators, currency symbols). No cross-currency amounts shown." },
    { "scenario": "A deduction results in negative net earnings for a pay period", "expected_behavior": "For CA employees, negative net pay is generally prohibited — deductions cannot reduce pay below minimum wage. For ID contractors, negative balance may carry forward but must be clearly disclosed with explanation." },
    { "scenario": "Data latency causes real-time earnings to show stale figures", "expected_behavior": "The display must show the timestamp of the last update. If data is more than a jurisdiction-configurable threshold stale, a warning must be shown rather than displaying potentially misleading numbers." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "High exposure in California: Labor Code §226 violations carry $50-$100 per employee per pay period in penalties, uncapped in class actions. Germany: works council injunctions can halt the feature entirely, wasting development investment. Indonesia: low legal risk but earnings transparency may improve driver retention, reducing recruitment costs.",
    "operational": "Three distinct earnings calculation engines are required — not one engine with three templates. Each jurisdiction has different deduction types, legal fields, and governance gates. The Germany works council approval process may take 2-6 months and is outside engineering's control.",
    "user": "Drivers gain trust and satisfaction through transparent pay. California drivers get legally mandated clarity. Indonesian contractors can verify platform fees are applied correctly. However, if deductions are higher than drivers expect, transparency could increase churn — the business must be prepared for this.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL: Treating this as a single global feature with jurisdiction flags is the highest-risk approach. Misclassification signals (showing employee deductions to contractors or vice versa) could be used as evidence in labor lawsuits. Germany rollout without works council approval is an immediate legal violation. Real-time display of preliminary figures could be cited as inaccurate wage statements if not properly disclaimed.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Drivers (California)", "impact": "Legally entitled to this information. Feature directly fulfills a statutory obligation. Errors expose them to incorrect pay and the company to penalties." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Germany)", "impact": "Cannot access the feature until works council approves. Delay is legally mandated and must be communicated transparently to avoid perception of unfairness." },
      { "group": "Drivers (Indonesia)", "impact": "Receive a transparency benefit with no legal mandate. Feature is a competitive differentiator for driver acquisition and retention." },
      { "group": "Works Council (Germany)", "impact": "Must be engaged as a stakeholder BEFORE development begins, not after. They may require design changes, data access limitations, or reject the proposal entirely." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Must review and sign off on each jurisdiction's implementation independently. Cannot treat this as one legal review." },
      { "group": "Finance/Payroll", "impact": "Real-time earnings display must be consistent with payroll calculations. Any discrepancy between what the driver sees in real-time and what appears on their paycheck will erode trust and generate support tickets." },
      { "group": "Platform/Product Team", "impact": "Must build jurisdiction-aware feature gating, not just internationalization. The Germany kill switch and approval gate are product-level concerns, not just legal footnotes." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 45,
    "reasoning": "This feature spans three jurisdictions with active legal/regulatory requirements (California Labor Code, German Works Constitution Act/GDPR, Indonesian labor law). No legal review has been performed. The spec says 'show earnings breakdown' but does not acknowledge that California's requirements make this a legally regulated document (itemized wage statement), that Germany requires pre-deployment works council approval (a potential multi-month blocker), or that mixing employee/contractor terminology creates misclassification risk. The spec treats this as one feature; it is three legally distinct features sharing a UI intent.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Tips must not offset base pay in California (well-established law)",
      "Works council approval is a hard prerequisite in Germany (fundamental BetrVG requirement)",
      "Driver data isolation is required across all jurisdictions",
      "Employee vs contractor deduction types must not cross-contaminate"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact §226 field requirements for real-time vs periodic statements (needs legal review)",
      "Whether Indonesian PDP Law imposes additional consent requirements for earnings display",
      "How Germany works council will interpret 'compensation display system' scope — they may require co-determination over the entire feature, not just the German portion",
      "Whether real-time display constitutes a 'wage statement' under California law or is legally distinct",
      "Minimum wage calculation methodology for AB5 drivers with mixed available/active time"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "California drivers are classified as employees under AB5 as stated — no pending reclassification",
      "The platform has established works councils in its German operations",
      "Indonesian independent contractor classification is legally defensible and not under challenge",
      "The platform already has jurisdiction data for each driver and does not need to infer it",
      "Payroll/finance systems can provide the data needed for real-time display with acceptable latency"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "The spec does not distinguish between real-time earnings display and legally mandated itemized wage statements. In California, an itemized wage statement is a regulated document with specific required fields (Labor Code §226). If the real-time display IS the wage statement, it must meet all §226 requirements at all times. If it is NOT the wage statement, its relationship to the official statement must be defined. This distinction fundamentally changes the feature's requirements.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (California)", "Legal/Compliance Team", "Product Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Real-time display is informational only; separate official wage statements are issued via payroll",
        "B: Real-time display serves as the itemized wage statement and must meet all §226 requirements at every moment"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — decouple the real-time transparency feature from the legal wage statement obligation. This limits legal exposure while still delivering driver value. But legal must confirm."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec lists 'deductions' as a single category but does not define what deductions apply in each jurisdiction. Employee deductions (tax withholding, social insurance, benefit premiums, garnishments) are fundamentally different from contractor deductions (platform fees, vehicle costs, insurance opt-ins). The deduction model must be jurisdiction-specific. What specific deduction types exist in each market?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (all jurisdictions)", "Finance/Payroll", "Legal/Compliance Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Define a fixed deduction taxonomy per jurisdiction now",
        "B: Build an extensible deduction-type registry that is configured per jurisdiction"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — deduction types will evolve as regulations change. But the initial taxonomy for each jurisdiction must be defined by legal and finance before development begins."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Germany works council engagement timeline is not addressed. The works council approval process can take 2-6 months and may result in design changes or rejection. Has the works council been consulted? Is there an existing agreement framework? This is a critical-path blocker for the Germany rollout.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Works Council (Germany)", "Drivers (Germany)", "Product Team", "Legal/Compliance Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Begin works council consultation immediately, in parallel with development for CA and ID",
        "B: Defer Germany rollout entirely until CA and ID are shipped and proven"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — begin consultation now. Works council process is calendar-time bound and cannot be compressed by throwing engineering resources at it."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec says 'real time' but does not define acceptable latency, update triggers, or what happens when data is delayed. Does 'real time' mean after each delivery settlement, after each delivery completion, or continuous streaming? The answer affects architecture, cost, and legal compliance.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Drivers (all jurisdictions)", "Engineering", "Product Team"],
      "options": [
        "A: Update after each delivery is completed and settled (event-driven, seconds-to-minutes latency)",
        "B: Continuous streaming updates during active delivery (higher cost, higher complexity)",
        "C: Near-real-time with periodic polling (simplest, but 'real time' may be misleading)"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — event-driven updates after delivery completion. This balances driver expectations with system complexity and ensures figures are based on settled transactions, not in-flight estimates."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
